content:
Summary: Implemented read-only Supabase-backed Health Records integration with one dedicated table per Health Records screen/category. Added SQL schema for medical_history_records, immunization_records, medical_consultation_records, and laboratory_result_records; added typed backend read-only module/endpoints; added Flutter API client, mapper, and data-loading wrapper; converted Health Records screens from embedded static entries to authenticated backend-loaded records. No create/receive endpoints were added, matching the user's request to handle creation later. Validation passed with backend npm run build and frontend flutter analyze.

Decisions:
1. [finished] Use one table per Health Records screen/category instead of a single shared health_records table because the user explicitly requested separate tables for each area.
2. [finished] Store variable per-record detail rows in a details_json JSONB array on each table so manual Supabase inserts remain simple while frontend details can still render label/value fields.
3. [finished] Implement read-only backend integration now and intentionally exclude create/receive DTOs or POST endpoints because the user plans to implement backend receiving later.

Affected files:
1. [created] sqls/007_health_records_setup.sql — Defines the four dedicated Health Records Supabase tables, indexes, updated_at triggers, and select-own RLS policies. | Notes: Must be applied manually to Supabase.
2. [modified] backend/src/supabase/database.types.ts — Adds typed Supabase table definitions for the four new Health Records tables and a Json helper type. | Notes: Keeps backend repository access type-safe.
3. [modified] backend/src/app.module.ts — Registers the new HealthRecordsModule in the NestJS application module. | Notes: Needed for the read endpoints to be available.
4. [created] backend/src/health-records/health-records.types.ts — Defines the backend Health Records response contracts, table-name union, section union, and row shape. | Notes: No create input types were added.
5. [created] backend/src/health-records/health-records.repository.ts — Provides read-only Supabase access and maps table rows plus details_json into typed Health Records responses. | Notes: Validates details_json by accepting only label/value string entries.
6. [created] backend/src/health-records/health-records.service.ts — Owns authenticated user resolution and maps sections to the correct dedicated table. | Notes: Keeps auth and table routing out of the controller.
7. [created] backend/src/health-records/health-records.controller.ts — Adds authenticated read-only GET endpoints for each Health Records screen/category. | Notes: No POST endpoints were added.
8. [created] backend/src/health-records/health-records.module.ts — Composes AuthModule, SupabaseModule, repository, service, and controller for the Health Records backend feature. | Notes: Follows Personal Records module separation.
9. [created] frontend/lib/features/health_records/data/health_records_api_client.dart — Adds a typed Flutter API client for authenticated read-only Health Records backend calls. | Notes: Uses existing backend base URL/API key config and bearer token pattern.
10. [created] frontend/lib/features/health_records/presentation/mappers/health_record_entry_mapper.dart — Maps backend HealthRecordResponse objects into existing HealthRecordEntry presentation models. | Notes: Keeps color/icon key translation out of screen widgets.
11. [created] frontend/lib/features/health_records/presentation/widgets/health_record_data_screen.dart — Adds the reusable data-loading boundary for Health Records screens while preserving the presentation template. | Notes: Handles loading, retryable error state, and authenticated token lookup.
12. [modified] frontend/lib/features/health_records/presentation/screens/medical_history_screen.dart — Converted from hardcoded entries to repository/API-backed HealthRecordDataScreen composition. | Notes: Keeps labels, filters, help copy, and empty copy.
13. [modified] frontend/lib/features/health_records/presentation/screens/immunization_records_screen.dart — Converted from hardcoded entries to repository/API-backed HealthRecordDataScreen composition. | Notes: Keeps labels, filters, help copy, and empty copy.
14. [modified] frontend/lib/features/health_records/presentation/screens/medical_consultations_screen.dart — Converted from hardcoded entries to repository/API-backed HealthRecordDataScreen composition. | Notes: Keeps labels, filters, help copy, and empty copy.
15. [modified] frontend/lib/features/health_records/presentation/screens/laboratory_results_screen.dart — Converted from hardcoded entries to repository/API-backed HealthRecordDataScreen composition. | Notes: Keeps labels, filters, help copy, and empty copy.

Added SQL schema for medical_history_records, immunization_records, medical_consultation_records, and laboratory_result_records; added typed backend read-only module/endpoints; added Flutter API client, mapper, and data-loading wrapper; converted Health Records screens from embedded static entries to authenticated backend-loaded records. No create/receive endpoints were added, matching the user's request to handle creation later. Validation passed with backend npm run build and frontend flutter analyze.

content:
Summary: Implemented read-only Supabase-backed Health Records storage integration. Added SQL schema for a single health_records table designed for manual Supabase inserts and future backend create endpoints, added backend database typings and a read-only NestJS HealthRecords module, and refactored Flutter Health Records screens to load authenticated records from the backend without adding any create/receive endpoints. Validation passed with backend npm run build and frontend flutter analyze.

Decisions:
1. [finished] Used one shared health_records table with a JSON details array instead of four section-specific tables because the existing UI has a common card/detail structure but variable detail fields.
2. [finished] Kept the Health Records integration read-only as requested, preparing schema and list/read flow only and intentionally omitting receiving/create DTOs and POST handlers.
3. [finished] Kept HealthRecordScreenTemplate as the presentation layer and added loading/error support rather than placing API logic in each individual screen.

Affected files:
1. [created] sqls/007_health_records_setup.sql — Defines the new Supabase Health Records table, constraints, indexes, trigger, and RLS policies for manual storage and future backend integration. | Notes: Must be applied to Supabase before live reads can return seeded data.
2. [modified] backend/src/supabase/database.types.ts — Adds typed Database metadata for the health_records table and a Json type used by details_json. | Notes: Keeps backend Supabase reads type-safe.
3. [created] backend/src/health-records/health-records.types.ts — Defines Health Records backend response contracts, section key validation, tone types, and details_json parsing. | Notes: No create input types were added.
4. [created] backend/src/health-records/health-records.repository.ts — Implements read-only Supabase querying and row-to-response mapping for Health Records. | Notes: Orders by display_order ascending then recorded_at descending.
5. [created] backend/src/health-records/health-records.service.ts — Owns auth-token user resolution and section-key validation before delegating reads to the repository. | Notes: Uses existing AuthSupportService.
6. [created] backend/src/health-records/health-records.controller.ts — Provides the GET /health-records endpoint with query-based section selection. | Notes: No receiving/create endpoint was added.
7. [created] backend/src/health-records/health-records.module.ts — Registers the Health Records controller, service, repository, AuthModule, and SupabaseModule. | Notes: Dedicated module preserves domain separation.
8. [modified] backend/src/app.module.ts — Imports and registers HealthRecordsModule in the NestJS application. | Notes: Keeps endpoint active in the backend app.
9. [created] frontend/lib/features/health_records/data/health_records_api_client.dart — Adds a typed Flutter API client for read-only Health Records list requests using existing backend config and bearer-token auth. | Notes: Handles timeout/backend errors and response parsing.
10. [created] frontend/lib/features/health_records/presentation/mappers/health_record_entry_mapper.dart — Maps backend HealthRecordResponse objects to existing HealthRecordEntry presentation models, including tone and icon key mapping. | Notes: Keeps API parsing separate from widget rendering.
11. [created] frontend/lib/features/health_records/presentation/screens/health_record_section_screen.dart — Shared stateful screen that loads records for a section and composes HealthRecordScreenTemplate with loading/error/retry states. | Notes: Prevents duplicated API loading logic across the four section screens.
12. [modified] frontend/lib/features/health_records/presentation/widgets/health_record_screen_template.dart — Adds reusable loading, error, and retry presentation states while preserving existing search/filter/list behavior. | Notes: Template remains presentation-only.
13. [modified] frontend/lib/features/health_records/presentation/screens/medical_history_screen.dart — Refactored from hardcoded record entries to a thin section configuration using section_key medical_history. | Notes: Existing labels/help/filter options preserved.
14. [modified] frontend/lib/features/health_records/presentation/screens/immunization_records_screen.dart — Refactored from hardcoded record entries to a thin section configuration using section_key immunization_records. | Notes: Existing labels/help/filter options preserved.
15. [modified] frontend/lib/features/health_records/presentation/screens/medical_consultations_screen.dart — Refactored from hardcoded record entries to a thin section configuration using section_key medical_consultations. | Notes: Existing labels/help/filter options preserved.
16. [modified] frontend/lib/features/health_records/presentation/screens/laboratory_results_screen.dart — Refactored from hardcoded record entries to a thin section configuration using section_key laboratory_results. | Notes: Existing labels/help/filter options preserved.

Added SQL schema for a single health_records table designed for manual Supabase inserts and future backend create endpoints, added backend database typings and a read-only NestJS HealthRecords module, and refactored Flutter Health Records screens to load authenticated records from the backend without adding any create/receive endpoints. Validation passed with backend npm run build and frontend flutter analyze.

content:
Summary: Reviewed the frontend Health Records screens and the existing Personal Records architecture to plan a future local persistence layer for Health Records. The current Health Records feature is still presentation-driven with hardcoded entry data in the screen files, while Personal Records already has a dedicated typed data/repository pattern. The recommended approach is to introduce a separate local health-records repository and domain model layer so the UI can read from a single source of truth now and later support creation without reworking the screens.

Decisions:
1. [ongoing] Use a separate local Health Records module instead of mixing storage logic into the dashboard screens or the presentation widgets, to keep responsibilities separated and future creation flows easy to add.
2. [ongoing] Prefer a single typed local repository/source-of-truth for all Health Records categories, with screen-specific view models mapped from shared record data rather than duplicating storage logic per screen.

Affected files:
1. [inspected] frontend/lib/features/dashboard/presentation/screens/health_records_screen.dart — This screen routes into the Health Records feature and shows the current user-facing hub that should remain presentation-only. | Notes: Reviewed navigation and help copy.
2. [inspected] frontend/lib/features/dashboard/presentation/screens/personal_records_screen.dart — This screen is the closest architectural reference for the Personal Records feature and its feature-entry pattern. | Notes: Reviewed the route targets used for personal records.
3. [inspected] frontend/lib/features/health_records/presentation/models/health_record_models.dart — This file defines the shared record entry and detail shapes that any local repository will need to feed. | Notes: Contains the core presentation models.
4. [inspected] frontend/lib/features/health_records/presentation/widgets/health_record_screen_template.dart — This widget owns search, filtering, and expansion behavior and should stay separate from storage concerns. | Notes: It is the primary screen composition layer.
5. [inspected] frontend/lib/features/health_records/presentation/screens/medical_history_screen.dart — This screen currently embeds hardcoded health record entries and is a primary candidate for repository-backed data. | Notes: Reviewed its static record list.
6. [inspected] frontend/lib/features/health_records/presentation/screens/immunization_records_screen.dart — This screen currently embeds hardcoded health record entries and is a primary candidate for repository-backed data. | Notes: Reviewed its static record list.
7. [inspected] frontend/lib/features/health_records/presentation/screens/medical_consultations_screen.dart — This screen currently embeds hardcoded health record entries and is a primary candidate for repository-backed data. | Notes: Reviewed its static record list.
8. [inspected] frontend/lib/features/health_records/presentation/screens/laboratory_results_screen.dart — This screen currently embeds hardcoded health record entries and is a primary candidate for repository-backed data. | Notes: Reviewed its static record list.
9. [inspected] frontend/lib/features/phr/data/personal_records_api_client.dart — This typed API client provides the strongest existing precedent for how record data should be modeled and loaded in a dedicated layer. | Notes: Used as the architecture comparison point.

The recommended approach is to introduce a separate local health-records repository and domain model layer so the UI can read from a single source of truth now and later support creation without reworking the screens.

content:
Summary: Wired the Personal Records screens to the new Supabase-backed backend. Added a dedicated backend PHR module with typed read/create endpoints for BMI, blood pressure, temperature, and medication intake, then updated the Flutter BMI, blood pressure, temperature, and medicine screens to load and save through the API using the authenticated access token. Verified with backend build success and frontend flutter analyze success.

Decisions:
1. [finished] Keep Personal Records in a dedicated backend module rather than folding them into auth or a generic monolith, to preserve separation by domain and screen responsibility.
2. [finished] Use authenticated bearer-token access from the Flutter app to the backend for PHR persistence, matching the existing auth API pattern.
3. [finished] Store medicine intake as history-style events with a snapshot in notes so the UI can reconstruct the original display fields without adding extra tables.

Affected files:
1. [modified] C:\Users\Administrator\Desktop\wah4patients\backend\src\app.module.ts — Registered the new PersonalRecordsModule so the backend exposes the PHR routes. | Notes: Imports now include the dedicated PHR module.
2. [modified] C:\Users\Administrator\Desktop\wah4patients\backend\src\auth\auth.module.ts — Exported AuthSupportService so the new PHR module can reuse authenticated user resolution. | Notes: Keeps auth boundary shared without duplicating token parsing.
3. [created] C:\Users\Administrator\Desktop\wah4patients\backend\src\personal-records\personal-records.module.ts — Introduced a dedicated module for PHR persistence and controllers. | Notes: Owns the new domain boundary.
4. [created] C:\Users\Administrator\Desktop\wah4patients\backend\src\personal-records\personal-records.controller.ts — Added typed /phr endpoints for list/create operations across all four record types. | Notes: Controller returns explicit promise types.
5. [created] C:\Users\Administrator\Desktop\wah4patients\backend\src\personal-records\personal-records.service.ts — Added orchestration between auth resolution and repository mapping for PHR operations. | Notes: Handles per-user access and request normalization.
6. [created] C:\Users\Administrator\Desktop\wah4patients\backend\src\personal-records\personal-records.repository.ts — Added typed Supabase read/write access for bmi_records, blood_pressure_records, temperature_records, and medication_intake_records. | Notes: Includes typed row mapping and insert helper.
7. [created] C:\Users\Administrator\Desktop\wah4patients\backend\src\personal-records\personal-records.types.ts — Defined backend response/input contracts for the new PHR endpoints. | Notes: Keeps API shapes explicit.

content:
Summary: Implemented the first Personal Records persistence slice with separate Supabase tables for BMI, blood pressure, temperature, and medicine intake. Added a new SQL migration, updated typed database contracts, and aligned the schema documentation and backend README with the new tables. Verified the backend still builds successfully after the type updates.

Decisions:
1. [finished] Separate tables were chosen for each Personal Records screen because the data shapes and validation rules differ and the app will be easier to query and maintain that way.
2. [finished] The new schema stores BMI, blood pressure, temperature, and medicine intake as first-class patient-owned records in Supabase instead of keeping them as session-only UI state.
3. [finished] Medication intake was modeled as an event/history table with a medication snapshot and scheduled/taken timestamps so history stays stable even if prescriptions change later.

Affected files:
1. [created] C:\Users\Administrator\Desktop\wah4patients\sqls\006_personal_records_setup.sql — Defines the new persistent database schema for personal records with separate tables, RLS, and indexes. | Notes: Includes BMI, blood pressure, temperature, and medication intake tables.
2. [modified] C:\Users\Administrator\Desktop\wah4patients\backend\src\supabase\database.types.ts — Adds strongly typed Supabase table contracts for the new personal record tables so backend data access remains type-safe. | Notes: Includes explicit row, insert, and update shapes for each table.
3. [modified] C:\Users\Administrator\Desktop\wah4patients\data-structure.md — Keeps the architecture document aligned with the implemented personal records schema and the new table-per-screen approach. | Notes: Updated the Personal Records section and BMI notes.
4. [modified] C:\Users\Administrator\Desktop\wah4patients\backend\README.md — Documents that the new personal records bootstrap script should be run alongside the auth/profile bootstrap script. | Notes: Helps keep setup instructions consistent with the new migration.

content:
Summary: Changed the profile personal information screen from a card-based layout to a flatter BMI-like page structure after the user clarified they wanted no card styling. The screen now uses a plain header, inline profile summary row, divider separation, and flat form sections while keeping the existing save/reset behavior intact.

Decisions:
1. [finished] The personal information screen should follow a flat BMI-style presentation instead of card-based containers to better unify the app UI.
2. [finished] The profile summary and edit form were kept in the same screen file as focused private widgets, but their visual containers were removed rather than introducing new shared layout abstractions.

Affected files:
1. [modified] frontend/lib/features/profile/presentation/screens/personal_information_screen.dart — Removed the card-like shells and replaced them with a flat header, summary row, divider, and form section. | Notes: Kept the behavior and compatibility parameters unchanged.
2. [modified] frontend/test/features/profile/presentation/screens/personal_information_screen_test.dart — Updated the test description to reflect the flat layout while preserving the main content assertions. | Notes: No functional assertions were removed.

The screen now uses a plain header, inline profile summary row, divider separation, and flat form sections while keeping the existing save/reset behavior intact.

content:
Summary: Updated the profile personal information screen to visually align with the BMI screen's card-based layout and header spacing, while preserving the existing profile hydration and save/reset behavior. Also updated the widget test to reflect the new card sections and verified the feature with flutter test and flutter analyze.

Decisions:
1. [finished] The profile personal information screen was restyled directly rather than extracting new shared layout components, because the change was scoped to one screen and could stay maintainable within the existing file.
2. [finished] The screen kept its existing optional showBackButton, wrapWithSafeArea, and centerContent parameters for backward compatibility while adopting the new BMI-inspired visual structure.

Affected files:
1. [modified] frontend/lib/features/profile/presentation/screens/personal_information_screen.dart — Restyled the screen to match the BMI screen's layout and card treatment while preserving its profile edit behavior. | Notes: Added private header, summary card, and form card widgets inside the file.
2. [modified] frontend/test/features/profile/presentation/screens/personal_information_screen_test.dart — Updated the widget test to reflect the new card-based rendering and helper text on the personal information screen. | Notes: Kept the same functional assertions for title, fields, and action buttons.

metadata.summary: Updated the profile personal information screen to visually align with the BMI screen's card-based layout and header spacing, while preserving the existing profile hydration and save/reset behavior. Also updated the widget test to reflect the new card sections and verified the feature with flutter test and flutter analyze.

content:
Summary: Fixed the Security Settings screen crash caused by calling ScaffoldMessenger.of(context) during initState-driven async hydration. The login redirect is now deferred with a post-frame callback, which keeps the cached preloaded Security screen behavior intact and avoids inherited-widget timing errors. Flutter analysis passed after the fix.

Decisions:
1. [finished] Keep the cache-based instant-load approach and fix the lifecycle crash by deferring login navigation instead of reverting the preload behavior.
2. [finished] Use ScaffoldMessenger.maybeOf(context) inside the deferred callback to avoid hard failing if the messenger is unavailable during redirect.

Affected files:
1. [modified] C:\Users\Administrator\Desktop\wah4patients\frontend\lib\features\profile\presentation\screens\security_settings_screen.dart — Changed the login redirect to run after the first frame so the screen no longer throws when initState-triggered async work calls ScaffoldMessenger too early. | Notes: The rest of the cache-first loading behavior remains intact.

content:
Summary: Added a plain-English atlas for WAH4P so future work can read the project like a map. The repo is best understood in layers: a Flutter client that controls navigation and presentation, a NestJS backend that owns security and business rules, and Supabase/Postgres plus secure local storage for persistence. The most important user journeys now have an explicit flow: splash/startup -> onboarding or login -> dashboard or MPIN unlock -> feature areas like records, appointments, resupply, calendar, notifications, profile, and legal screens. This memory also clarifies which screens are static, which are route-driven, and which backend APIs support each major journey.

Decisions:
1. [finished] Use the plain-English layer model for future reasoning: backend = rules and APIs, frontend = screens and navigation, database/docs = persistence and domain model.
2. [finished] When updating routes or screens, treat the route constants and router as the source of truth and update the screen groups from there instead of relying on older inventory documents.
3. [finished] Keep launch logic centralized in the app shell and startup service so startup routing stays easy to understand and does not leak into individual screens.
4. [finished] Keep backend auth responsibilities split by workflow so each part of the account journey remains testable and easy to extend.

Affected files:
1. [inspected] C:\Users\Administrator\Desktop\wah4patients\frontend\lib\app\wah4p_app.dart — The app shell controls theme, startup gating, and resume-time locking, making it the top-level navigation anchor for the project map. | Notes: Central entry point for the Flutter client.
2. [inspected] C:\Users\Administrator\Desktop\wah4patients\frontend\lib\app\app_startup_service.dart — Defines the route decision logic for onboarding, login, dashboard, and MPIN unlock, which is essential to the launch map. | Notes: Startup flow source of truth.
3. [inspected] C:\Users\Administrator\Desktop\wah4patients\frontend\lib\app\app_routes.dart — Lists the canonical route names used throughout the Flutter app. | Notes: Best place to understand route coverage.
4. [inspected] C:\Users\Administrator\Desktop\wah4patients\frontend\lib\app\app_router.dart — Maps route names to screens and shows transition style choices for each user flow. | Notes: Best source for the screen-to-route atlas.
5. [inspected] C:\Users\Administrator\Desktop\wah4patients\frontend\lib\app\startup_gate_screen.dart — Shows the transient gate screen that hands off to the resolved initial route during startup. | Notes: Launch UX and routing handoff.
6. [inspected] C:\Users\Administrator\Desktop\wah4patients\frontend\lib\app\app_lock_state_service.dart — Implements the time-based unlock policy used when the app resumes from background. | Notes: Important for security and session behavior.
7. [inspected] C:\Users\Administrator\Desktop\wah4patients\frontend\lib\features — The feature directory structure reveals the major product areas and how the client is partitioned by domain. | Notes: Useful for understanding the app map by feature group.
8. [inspected] C:\Users\Administrator\Desktop\wah4patients\backend\src\app.module.ts — Shows the backend’s top-level composition, including global guards and core modules. | Notes: Server architecture anchor.
9. [inspected] C:\Users\Administrator\Desktop\wah4patients\backend\src\auth\auth.module.ts — Shows the backend auth domain composition across controllers, services, and repositories. | Notes: Primary auth subsystem map.
10. [inspected] C:\Users\Administrator\Desktop\wah4patients\backend\src\auth\auth.controller.ts — Exposes the auth workflow endpoints and groups them into clear account/security journeys. | Notes: API surface map for auth.
11. [inspected] C:\Users\Administrator\Desktop\wah4patients\backend\src\auth\profile.controller.ts — Provides authenticated profile read/update endpoints separate from the workflow controller. | Notes: Profile API map.
12. [inspected] C:\Users\Administrator\Desktop\wah4patients\backend\src\health\health.controller.ts — Shows the public health check route and the small public surface of the server. | Notes: Operational entry point.
13. [inspected] C:\Users\Administrator\Desktop\wah4patients\data-structure.md — Defines persistence rules, canonical entities, and the source-of-truth model used by the app. | Notes: Data/storage map.
14. [inspected] C:\Users\Administrator\Desktop\wah4patients\checklist.md — Tracks unfinished persistence and validation work across the product and shows which areas still need implementation. | Notes: Project readiness map.
15. [inspected] C:\Users\Administrator\Desktop\wah4patients\SCREENS.md — Documents screen inventory and route flow, though it is older and should be reconciled with the live code. | Notes: Secondary reference, not authoritative.

The repo is best understood in layers: a Flutter client that controls navigation and presentation, a NestJS backend that owns security and business rules, and Supabase/Postgres plus secure local storage for persistence. The most important user journeys now have an explicit flow: splash/startup -> onboarding or login -> dashboard or MPIN unlock -> feature areas like records, appointments, resupply, calendar, notifications, profile, and legal screens. content:
Summary: Saved a deeper project map for WAH4P that focuses on navigation, feature boundaries, and service responsibilities. The repository presents a clear two-tier architecture: a NestJS backend that owns auth/security/business rules and a Flutter frontend that owns display, navigation, and device-scoped state. The frontend route tree now has a precise map from startup through onboarding, auth, dashboard, PHR, EHR-style records, appointments, calendar, notifications, profile, and legal screens. The backend API surface is also clearer: a public health check plus a broad auth/profile domain that covers registration, login, 2FA, MPIN, password reset, and profile hydration/update. This memory layer is intended to function as an app map, not just a summary.

Decisions:
1. [finished] Treat backend code as the authority for security, validation, auth workflows, and persistence, and treat the Flutter client as the presentation/navigation layer that consumes those backend capabilities.
2. [finished] Use the new app map as a navigation reference: startup -> onboarding/login/dashboard/unlock, then the dashboard branches into health records, personal records, appointments, medication resupply, calendar, notifications, profile, and security flows.
3. [finished] Keep the backend auth domain split by workflow rather than merging all auth logic into one service: registration, login, password reset, 2FA/TOTP, MPIN, and profile handling remain separate responsibilities.
4. [finished] Preserve the current startup design: the app should decide the initial route from auth/onboarding/device state before the user reaches the main dashboard shell.
5. [finished] Treat the older screen inventory documents as useful but secondary; if they conflict with the live code, the route builder and app route constants win as the authoritative map.

Affected files:
1. [inspected] C:\Users\Administrator\Desktop\wah4patients\frontend\lib\app\wah4p_app.dart — Defines the Flutter app shell, theme, route entrypoint, and resume-time lock behavior, which are central to the app map. | Notes: Confirms where app-wide navigation and lifecycle control live.
2. [inspected] C:\Users\Administrator\Desktop\wah4patients\frontend\lib\app\app_router.dart — Contains the full named route registry and screen-to-route mapping for the Flutter client. | Notes: This is the best source of truth for the app’s visible navigation tree.
3. [inspected] C:\Users\Administrator\Desktop\wah4patients\frontend\lib\app\app_routes.dart — Lists every canonical route constant used by the Flutter app. | Notes: Useful for maintaining route names and screen grouping.
4. [inspected] C:\Users\Administrator\Desktop\wah4patients\frontend\lib\app\startup_gate_screen.dart — Shows how the app waits for startup resolution and then redirects to the correct initial route. | Notes: Important for understanding the launch flow.
5. [inspected] C:\Users\Administrator\Desktop\wah4patients\frontend\lib\app\app_startup_service.dart — Implements startup routing decisions from auth, onboarding, and MPIN state. | Notes: This is the decision engine behind splash-to-app routing.
6. [inspected] C:\Users\Administrator\Desktop\wah4patients\frontend\lib\app\app_lock_state_service.dart — Implements the app’s background timing and unlock requirement policy. | Notes: Key to understanding resume/lock behavior.
7. [inspected] C:\Users\Administrator\Desktop\wah4patients\frontend\lib\features — Directory structure reveals the major presentation feature families that make up the client. | Notes: Confirms the app is organized by domain feature.
8. [inspected] C:\Users\Administrator\Desktop\wah4patients\backend\src\app.module.ts — Defines backend module composition and global guards, which frame the server-side architecture map. | Notes: Shows the core backend wiring.
9. [inspected] C:\Users\Administrator\Desktop\wah4patients\backend\src\auth\auth.module.ts — Shows how the backend auth domain is composed from controllers, services, and repositories. | Notes: Critical for understanding auth responsibility split.
10. [inspected] C:\Users\Administrator\Desktop\wah4patients\backend\src\auth\auth.controller.ts — Lists the auth workflow endpoints and groups them into registration, login, 2FA, security, and MPIN routes. | Notes: Primary backend workflow surface.
11. [inspected] C:\Users\Administrator\Desktop\wah4patients\backend\src\auth\profile.controller.ts — Exposes profile hydration and update endpoints separate from the auth workflow controller. | Notes: Important for profile screen integration.
12. [inspected] C:\Users\Administrator\Desktop\wah4patients\backend\src\health\health.controller.ts — Provides the public health endpoint and marks the small public server surface. | Notes: Useful for operational checks.

The repository presents a clear two-tier architecture: a NestJS backend that owns auth/security/business rules and a Flutter frontend that owns display, navigation, and device-scoped state. The frontend route tree now has a precise map from startup through onboarding, auth, dashboard, PHR, EHR-style records, appointments, calendar, notifications, profile, and legal screens. content:
Summary: Audited the WAH4P repository and saved a clear project snapshot for future work. The repo is split into a NestJS backend, a Flutter frontend, and supporting SQL/docs. The backend is a secured API with global API-key protection, throttling, strict env validation, Supabase auth/data integration, and Resend email delivery. The frontend is a Flutter mobile app with centralized theming, secure local storage, a startup gate, and MPIN/session lifecycle handling. The docs describe a Supabase/Postgres source of truth for patient data, with frontend screens mostly acting as presentation and interaction layers. I also captured the current testing surface and a couple of documentation mismatches that future work should reconcile.

Decisions:
1. [finished] Keep backend responsibilities authoritative for business logic, security, and persistence, while treating the frontend as a display-and-interaction layer that consumes backend data.
2. [finished] Use Supabase/Postgres as the canonical store for patient and auth records, and keep local secure storage limited to session, onboarding, and device-bound security data.
3. [finished] Preserve the app-shell pattern in the Flutter client: centralized theme and startup routing in the app entrypoint, with feature-specific screens and widgets split by responsibility.
4. [finished] Treat the audited docs as guidance rather than immutable truth when they conflict with the live source tree, especially for screen inventories and path references.

Affected files:
1. [inspected] C:\Users\Administrator\Desktop\wah4patients\AGENTS.md — Contains repository-wide operating rules, architecture reminders, and documentation guidance that shape how the project should be understood. | Notes: Established the backend/frontend responsibility split and project conventions.
2. [inspected] C:\Users\Administrator\Desktop\wah4patients\data-structure.md — Defines the canonical data model, storage layers, and screen-to-data mapping used by the app. | Notes: Important for understanding persistence and source-of-truth rules.
3. [inspected] C:\Users\Administrator\Desktop\wah4patients\checklist.md — Lists the current persistence and validation work items across the app and acts as a project readiness tracker. | Notes: Useful for identifying unfinished foundation work.
4. [inspected] C:\Users\Administrator\Desktop\wah4patients\SCREENS.md — Documents the screen inventory and route mapping, which is critical for understanding the frontend surface area. | Notes: Contains a likely path mismatch with the live repo layout.
5. [inspected] C:\Users\Administrator\Desktop\wah4patients\backend\package.json — Shows the backend stack, scripts, test tooling, and key runtime dependencies. | Notes: Confirms NestJS, Supabase, Resend, Jest, and validation tooling.
6. [inspected] C:\Users\Administrator\Desktop\wah4patients\backend\README.md — Summarizes backend setup, auth endpoints, SQL bootstrap, and local development steps. | Notes: Useful for capturing the intended API surface.
7. [inspected] C:\Users\Administrator\Desktop\wah4patients\backend\src\main.ts — Shows backend bootstrap behavior, including security middleware, route prefixing, validation, and CORS configuration. | Notes: Establishes the HTTP entrypoint behavior.
8. [inspected] C:\Users\Administrator\Desktop\wah4patients\backend\src\app.module.ts — Defines the backend module composition, including global guards and configuration wiring. | Notes: Confirms API-key and throttling guards are global.
9. [inspected] C:\Users\Administrator\Desktop\wah4patients\backend\src\common\config\env.validation.ts — Lists the required environment variables and validation constraints for the backend. | Notes: Critical for deployment and security readiness.
10. [inspected] C:\Users\Administrator\Desktop\wah4patients\backend\src\common\guards\api-key.guard.ts — Shows how the backend enforces the x-api-key header and compares secrets safely. | Notes: Important security boundary for all non-public routes.
11. [inspected] C:\Users\Administrator\Desktop\wah4patients\frontend\README.md — Documents runtime configuration for the Flutter client and how it connects to the backend. | Notes: Confirms the app uses backend URL and API key configuration.
12. [inspected] C:\Users\Administrator\Desktop\wah4patients\frontend\pubspec.yaml — Shows the Flutter SDK version, package dependencies, and bundled assets used by the app. | Notes: Confirms secure storage, HTTP, QR, and local asset usage.
13. [inspected] C:\Users\Administrator\Desktop\wah4patients\frontend\analysis_options.yaml — Captures the Flutter lint baseline for the client codebase. | Notes: No custom lint overrides beyond the default Flutter lint set.
14. [inspected] C:\Users\Administrator\Desktop\wah4patients\frontend\lib\main.dart — Shows the Flutter application entrypoint and how the app widget is launched. | Notes: Thin entrypoint that delegates to the app shell.
15. [inspected] C:\Users\Administrator\Desktop\wah4patients\frontend\lib\app\wah4p_app.dart — Defines the Flutter app shell, theme, route generation, startup gate, and MPIN lock-on-resume behavior. | Notes: Central file for app lifecycle and navigation policy.

The frontend is a Flutter mobile app with centralized theming, secure local storage, a startup gate, and MPIN/session lifecycle handling.
